headline: AI products need a different lifecycle: deploy is not a finish line, agency is earned over time
digest_md:
Aishwarya Reganti and Kiriti Badam — having shipped AI features at OpenAI, Google, Amazon, Databricks, and Kumo, and consulted with 50+ companies — argue that PMs are dragging traditional-software muscle into a domain where it breaks. Their framework: **Continuous Calibration / Continuous Development (CC/CD).**

The two assumptions that trip up most teams:

1. **AI products are inherently non-deterministic.** *'AI models are trained to generate plausible responses based on patterns, not to follow fixed rules. The same request can produce different results depending on phrasing, context, or even a different model.'* You're no longer designing predictable user flows; you're designing for *likely* behavior.
2. **Every AI product negotiates a tradeoff between agency and control.** Too much agency too fast erodes trust; too much control kills the magic. *'Every new capability shifts control further away from humans.'*

The loop:

- **Development (CD)** — scope the problem, design architecture, set up evaluations to keep non-determinism in check. *'Start with features that are low-agency and high-control, then gradually move up as the system proves it can handle more.'*
- **Deploy** — not a finish line. A transition.
- **Calibration (CC)** — observe real behavior, figure out what broke, ship targeted fixes. Every cycle earns the system a bit more agency.

The failure mode they keep seeing: a great demo, glowing early reviews, then *'a system that can't scale or sustain'* — and user trust quietly erodes before the team realizes it's happening.

For a mid-level PM, the practical translation: stop writing PRDs that end at launch, start with the smallest agency surface you can defend, build evals before shipping, and treat post-launch calibration as the actual job, not a bug-fix backlog.
takeaways: Your next AI PRD should include evaluations alongside scope and architecture. If you're shipping without evals, you're shipping without a way to catch the regressions you can't predict.
Pick the lowest-agency version of your AI feature for v1. Earn agency cycle by cycle as the system proves it. The 'human-in-the-loop' version isn't a stepping stone; it's the right v1.
Replace 'launch readiness' with 'calibration plan.' Deploy is the start of work, not the end. Block calendar time for the first 4 weeks post-launch to observe real behavior.
If your AI feature looks great in demo but is wobbling in production, you have a non-determinism problem, not a bug. Treat the demo-to-production gap as predictable, not embarrassing.

source.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle

questions.scenario_md:
Your AI summarization feature demoed beautifully two months ago — execs were impressed, you got the green light. Now in production: a third of users report 'inconsistent' results, your support queue is filling, and your engineer says nothing is broken. Your VP asks for a status read.

Per the CC/CD framing, the most accurate diagnosis is —

options.text: Engineering quality issue — schedule a code review and ticket the inconsistencies as bugs.
User-education issue — write better in-product copy explaining how to phrase prompts.
Predictable demo-to-production drift from non-determinism; the missing piece is a calibration loop, not a bug fix.
Model regression — escalate to your foundation-model vendor.

questions.explanation_md: The CC/CD framework calls this the predictable failure mode: an impressive demo gives way to a system that can't scale, and user trust erodes quietly. The fix is not bug-triage — it's standing up a calibration loop that observes real behavior and ships targeted improvements.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week, set up a weekly calibration review for your AI feature: real outputs vs. expected, ship one targeted fix per cycle. Treat it as the work, not the cleanup.

citation.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
citation.quote_excerpt: We’ve seen so many teams experience the painful shift from an impressive demo to a system that can’t scale or sustain. And along the way, user trust in the product quietly erodes.

questions.scenario_md:
You're scoping v1 of an AI inbox-triage feature. Four candidate behaviors are on the table:

1. Auto-reply, auto-send, auto-archive — fully autonomous.
2. Suggest a draft reply, surface in a sidebar; user clicks send.
3. Classify only — label emails by intent; user takes action.
4. Do nothing user-visible; just train on user actions for a future model.

Per the CC/CD framework, the v1 you ship is —

options.text: Option 1 — full autonomy maximizes the AI's value proposition.
Option 4 — collect data first, ship behavior later when the model is mature.
Option 2 — suggested drafts are the standard AI-assistant pattern.
Option 3 — low-agency, high-control surface; earn agency by proving the labels are reliable before adding actions.

questions.explanation_md: The framework's principle: agency must be earned over time, not granted all at once, because every new capability shifts control further away from humans. The right v1 is the lowest-agency surface that still teaches you what works — classification before suggestion, suggestion before action.
questions.pm_takeaway: For your next AI feature, write down the agency ladder explicitly: v1 (lowest), v2, v3 — and ship v1, not v2 or v3, this quarter.

citation.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
citation.quote_excerpt: Ensuring that agency is earned over time, not granted all at once, because every new capability shifts control further away from humans

questions.scenario_md:
An engineer on your team writes a unit-test plan for the AI feature you're shipping: 50 prompts, expected exact-match outputs, fail the build if any output drifts. They want to lock the test-suite in CI before launch. You're the PM and they ask if you're aligned.

Per the CC/CD framing, the missing step in their plan is —

options.text: Increase the test count to 500 prompts to reduce false negatives.
Add an evaluation regime that scores *likely* behavior across a distribution, not exact-match outputs — and define what 'good enough' looks like.
Move the test suite to a nightly run instead of CI to reduce build time.
Keep the test plan but allow a 5% mismatch tolerance to account for stochastic outputs.

questions.explanation_md: AI outputs are non-deterministic — the same request can produce different results based on phrasing, context, or model version. Exact-match unit tests fail this reality. The framework's answer is evaluations that score against a distribution of likely behavior, with explicit thresholds for 'good enough.'
questions.pm_takeaway: Before your next AI launch, write the eval rubric with your engineer: what's measured, what threshold means ship-ready, what means stop. No eval rubric = no launch.

citation.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
citation.quote_excerpt: AI models are trained to generate plausible responses based on patterns, not to follow fixed rules. The same request can produce different results depending on phrasing, context, or even a different model.

questions.scenario_md:
Your team's PRD template ends with a section called 'Launch Readiness Checklist.' You're rewriting the template for an AI feature shipping next month. You have one revision pass.

The revision that best translates the CC/CD framework to your PRD template is —

options.text: Replace it with a 'Calibration Plan' section: weekly observation cadence, eval thresholds, who ships fixes, when agency expands.
Rename the section to 'Launch + Day-One Bug Triage'.
Add an 'AI Risks' section listing hallucination scenarios for legal and security review.
Remove the Launch Readiness section entirely; AI features should ship continuously without checkpoints.

questions.explanation_md: The CC/CD framework treats deploy as a transition, not a finish line. The PRD-template translation is to replace 'launch readiness' with a structured calibration plan that defines what observation, what eval thresholds, and what agency progression look like post-launch.
questions.pm_takeaway: Edit your PRD template this week to add a Calibration Plan section. Use it on your next AI feature. Keep the agency progression explicit.

citation.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
citation.quote_excerpt: Then you deploy, not as a finish line but as a transition into the next phase. Once you’ve deployed, you enter the calibration loop, where you observe real behavior, figure out what broke, and make targeted improvements.

questions.scenario_md:
Your CEO Slacks: 'Make the AI agent fully autonomous — competitors are shipping autonomous mode.' You're the PM. Your current product surfaces AI suggestions that the user accepts or rejects. Your support data shows users sometimes mis-accept suggestions, but rarely catastrophically.

Applying the agency-control tradeoff, the response you ship is —

options.text: Ship full autonomy this quarter; competitors are setting the bar.
Reply: agency is earned, not granted. Propose an explicit ladder — current suggest-mode → batch-confirm → fully autonomous — gated by calibration metrics.
Build full autonomy behind a feature flag for power users only.
Push back; the current product is fine and competitor moves are noise.

questions.explanation_md: The framework's central tradeoff: every new capability shifts control away from humans, and giving agency too fast is how trust erodes. The PM-agency move is to write the explicit ladder with calibration gates — not to capitulate, hide it behind a flag, or refuse outright.
questions.pm_takeaway: Tomorrow, draft the agency ladder for your AI feature with named calibration thresholds for each step, and bring it to your next CEO check-in.

citation.title: Why your AI product needs a different development lifecycle
citation.quote_excerpt: Every AI product must negotiate a tradeoff between *agency* and *control*
